speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane. This is tape five. Ruth, you were talking about your work in the factory in Bedzin. And you said you had to be treated and work like an adult. What work did you do in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were finishing uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it wasn't-- German uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: For which-- do you know which unit they were for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. I don't remember that it was. This had to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- Oh. Like they wore. You know? Sort of like greenish or brownish something. They were various colors because the Wehrmacht sometimes wore a different-- they wore different hats. You know? They wore a different-- Obersturmbannfuhrer would have-- which was the highest, you know? And we had to-- we-- we were-- it was made. They were just-- we were finishing it. So it's not that the work was really-- that it was very hard, but you had to do it, again, with precision. So you had to total. You know the Germans, everything had to be perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you couldn't just get away with missing a stitch. And everything was hand, really. From what we did, the finishing was hand-finished. From what I did, you know? I couldn't operate a machine or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time it reached me, it was, you know, hand-finished. And I was-- I was the last person that would know how to sew. But I still had to do it perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was from early in the morning till late at night, so I really didn't see, you know? I-- at that stage, I didn't know that-- that maybe subconsciously, I still looked for inspiration. You know? Because that was the time, really, that I-- that i had a few books at my disposal. And you know, so I really could read something at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that was a time where, somehow, my parents made sure that it was available. So I was not aware, though, that-- that the inspiration-- at that stage, that the inspiration and that spiritual part. And I was too young for all of this. It came a bit, you know, later, where it was a conscious awareness of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if someone did do some work that was faulty, and it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, nobody ever did. You see, this was the whole thing. That nobody ever did. Because you knew if you did something which was faulty, the rest, they had to follow of what you were doing. You did this part, they did the other part. If yours wasn't right, and they-- they couldn't make-- finish it in the right way. Yes? It would have to be undone.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is they way-- on the left side? You know, this is in the way that it's done. It had to be perfect. If you have got no choice, if you know-- I wasn't conscious that my life depends on it, but by that time, I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that if we're going to be punished, we're going to be punished collectively. Not just I will be punished for my work, but by the same token, I assume that the others knew the same thing. So it had to be perfect. So you didn't find anything that was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a privately owned factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- once it was a private. I am not from Bedzin, so it's only people that are in Bedzin. If you will meet-- if you will talk to my sister-in-law, who agreed to do an interview, she will tell you exactly where, because she is from Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I thought that it is Rossner. I thought it was Rossner, but my sister-in-law says that-- says that-- states that it was something else. It was a different factory. I thought it was Rossner. But at that stage, again, you know, I was uprooted. Again, I didn't think about this then. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still felt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were selections, and there were all kinds of things. One day, they had to-- had a number of people, every day or every week, where they needed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to supplement the people. People-- people had to be there to send them away, because the aim was not to-- for us to live in Bedzin, to work there, but for us to-- to perish. You know? To perish. So they took away people. Let's say, like, they came to look for me one day, maybe when I was there about six weeks. I was on the list. And they came to look for me. And my mother realized for what it is. She said, go run upstairs and hide. I could tell you about miracles upon miracle, but go upstairs and hide. There are some old racks up-- there was something, like, on the roof, where they used to-- they used to dry the washing. You know? So she said, hang-- go and go behind it. And they came up and looking, and they looked around. And I wasn't there. But they took my mother, instead. At that time, I was not there, so they took my mother instead. What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to her, she would have been sent away, but somehow, because of my father, they released her. But then, they were all crying bitterly because the quota of people had to be dead. So you see, today was you, tomorrow was somebody else. It was-- it was sometimes-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's very difficult for me to tell you this. But that was a time where, in the ghetto itself, they didn't need the Germans. Because you know, there are a few Jewish people who were-- then, you know, who could round up. Who could round up. That's the only thing, I-- as far as I know-- as far as hurt me. And this is that there were some Jewish people that they thought that they're going to save their lives. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were from Bedzin. I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all-- no. No. That's-- but they were wearing something else. They were-- they were-- they were just people. They were-- if they wouldn't do it, they would be shot on the spot. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we knew at this stage-- what I didn't know, but I think that my parents knew, that at that stage, it seemed that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if we can save each other, we're going to save each other. Just like when we were rounded up. I wait for your question. Please, carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, when were rounded up in that factory, yes? When we were rounded up, one morning-- I think it was on a Tuesday-- when we were rounded up, we were all startled. We heard those sounds, which we were familiar with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come out, and you have to be counted, and things like this. I hid. I could see something else was in it. I already went through the liquidation of the ghetto. The other people didn't. You see? The Olkusz ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Olkusz ghetto. So I already knew that something is happening. But I hid in the beginning. But then I thought, no. I mean, they know how many people are working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're going to-- you know, they're going to go with bayonets, what you call it. You know? They had something there that sometimes, you know, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they could-- sort of-- a knife, anything. So I went out. And this is when they took us all-- that was after nearly three months in Bedzin. They took us all to dulag, to durchgangslager. Say that again, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Durchgangslager. Dulag, D-U-L-A-G. Dulag, in short.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Sosnowitz. That was like a camp, a temporary camp. Now, girls and boys were coming there from everywhere, and there were always hundreds and hundreds of them. And what I didn't know then, and what my father, apparently, tried to do, he tried to-- when they found out, he tried to get me out of there because one lady told-- one lady who survived, who saw my parents after I was taken. This is what she remembers vividly. She remembers my parents and my little sister and what my mother said to her very vividly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that my father was trying to get me out. But because we were picked at random-- yes? I mean, we had that zahlappell, a roll call. We all stood there, and came then. Came Dr. Mengele. This is the only time I saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had this whip, you know. And he was whipping, you know, going through his-- his polish. The shoes were so highly polished. You know? This is what I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't look them in the face. You couldn't make any eye contact. You couldn't, you know. The-- I only when-- I only stole sideways glances, because, to me, all along, looking at faces was the most important thing. What I perceived in faces-- if there was warmth, if there was a smile, if there was-- you know, this-- I didn't realize this, but this was something, which-- perhaps of my age, or perhaps this is the way I was. I don't know. But I couldn't look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the dulag. That was already-- we were rounded up and taken to the durchgangslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that this man was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I heard. I heard him being addressed, Dr. Mengele. I heard-- this is the only time. He had his like a whole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] group of people. It is a certain name in German that it is-- that it's called. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ensemble, like ensemble, you know, of people, like something. Obersturmbannfurher, whatever. You know? He had all those big names flying. But what stayed with me, I don't know the others. It might have been somebody who featured very prominently between the Gestapo and between the SS. But his name, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- he just pointed. And this day-- I don't know before, I don't know after-- this day, he just pointed at random. You and du, du, du, du. He just-- and I was between them. And this is how I was there, not even two days, something like this, and I was taken. Some of them, something happened. Maybe they were still sent home. Some of them, they were exchanged for older, whatever. I was there. That meant that I was old enough. Although, really, because I happened to be at a certain time at a certain place, that's why I was taken to this camp. And that was Oderberg, that was a-- a forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who selected you to go Oderberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Dr. Mengele there, and there were others. So it was with others, as well. They went to other camps. But there were seven girls that were chosen. And they were all from Bedzin. One girls was from Dombrowa, the oldest. Six were from Bedzin, and the oldest was from Dombrowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dombrowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. D-O-M-R-O-W-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these selections by Mengele, when he was saying, du, du--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He might have been there only in passing. I don't know. But this was when I was selected. He was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he select you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not look at his face. I just saw the shoes. So before he selected me, I heard his name. Yes? And so this is how-- and I heard other names. So I can't tell you. I wouldn't want to tell you something if I don't-- I am not sure 100%. And I know-- I just know that he was there. And I stole a glance at him. But most of the time-- and I stole a glance at the others. I don't remember the others' names, but I remember the highly polished boots. It was incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you stole a glance at him. Do you remember what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I stole, sideways, a glance. No, I don't remember, really. I just-- you know, at that stage, they all had-- just that he was tall, and that he was very impatient. I just remember, you know, walking backwards and forwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the sound of the whip. You know? When the whip went through the air. And he-- he whipped his boots. You know? He went [INAUDIBLE] over his boots. You know, when he spoke, and you heard Der Fuhrer [INAUDIBLE] die Juden. And you heard all kinds of things. You know? And then-- and then we were-- we were-- the seven girls were chosen. And I am not really sure, at that stage, how many men. But I'm only aware, really, at that stage, that the seven girls were chosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember even from this-- from this-- from the dulag. I remember there was one girl there whom I met who went to a different camp. But we met in the last camp. She came later on. Yes. So there were girls-- look, that was a time where your memory was, you know. You were young and all of this. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the journey from the dulag in Sosnowitz to Oderberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know much about the journey at all. I think we were just shoved, a lot of us, into-- into wagons. And the journey itself-- I think all of this is really-- we were just taken. They-- they-- we were so-- you know, we were just like sardines. You know? With other men. I don't-- as matter of fact, I haven't met anyone who survived Oderberg. A few girls did, but none of the men I have ever met that would have told me-- when we, in the beginning, were in other camps-- that would have told me that he was in Oderberg. So I can't say about the men. But the girls, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you how long the trip from Sosnowitz to Oderberg took?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't. But it was-- it was not a very long trip. But it was-- look, that was a time when the trains started and stopped and stalled. You know? It wasn't like it went fast. You know? So it was meant to confuse you as well. It was meant for you not to know when you-- where you are taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of the-- you-- if you listen to testimonies, you will see that a lot of people really didn't know. You know? Your mind really can't. You-- you are not so fresh and so eager to learn, either, you know? Where and what and how. You are barely-- by that stage, you are really barely surviving. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was, for me, it was like a total end. I mean, for me, it was a total separation. I mean, I was 14, and suddenly, I was in a place where my parents were not there. My sister was not there. Nobody was there. I didn't know anybody. And I didn't want it. I wanted-- whatever it was, if it was with my family, this is what I wanted. And my father promised me. Then suddenly, I felt, but my dad! But how could-- but my dad promised!

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was this is the way we were brought up, you see? No matter what you saw. I mean, your parents were everything, as far as I knew. Your family was everything. Even I remember, because we lived at such close proximity. So I remember-- I don't remember anybody talking back. Your parents said something, and you know. Perhaps if you were grown up, and if you-- first, the children would not have heard. The children were-- no matter what, the children were cushioned. There were protected. You know? So what you saw, if it had to do something with faith, if it had to do with what was allowed or was not allowed, I really don't know. But that's how it was. You said your father promised. What did he promise?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father promised that we will never be separated. In Olkusz, during the separation, I think he was so overwhelmed, you know, that he had us all together, and he wanted-- at that stage, when he said this to me, he didn't know yet what is going to happen. But he sort of was willing it to happen, that we will leave Olkusz, that we won't be separated. But it took me a long time to acknowledge that it was, even-- even him, who I refused to believe that he couldn't handle a situation. But that even him, who meant everything to me, who always kept his promises-- my parents always-- they didn't say anything that they couldn't promise.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then he didn't keep his-- he didn't keep his promise. And that was this part which is still in me. This part-- the longing is there. This is something that you-- I accept it, and I acknowledge it, that I might have grown. Growth, personal growth was very important to me later on. And I know you have to work on it. It's hard work. You have to face yourself and face your surroundings. But this part, I allow. This child within me, this part where it was the separation, where it was a very difficult-- this, I realized right there and then. You know? And I didn't want to. Not-- I didn't-- everything locked-- locked in me. I couldn't swallow anything. You know? I couldn't-- I didn't know anybody. I didn't-- I didn't want to. I-- I'm not saying that I was-- I don't think so my spirit was broken, just my physical. The physical self couldn't cope with it anymore. You know? As we will see later on, apparently the spiritual part, you know, apparently came to the fore. Because a few days later, I don't know how it happened, because there was nothing there to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there's no way a Jewish girl, you could ever-- on the bank that I-- that I was there, and I-- I-- it was, really, the finish of me. But that was the first days where they-- this camp was not established. Oderberg was not established when we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oderberg. O-D-E-R-B-E-R-G, Yes? Oderberg. And this was not established. We just came. That was-- there was just like a little barracks and another two barracks, and there was a little building where it was, later on, the kitchen. And there was two sort of like another two barracks where-- they were the German quarters. And there were-- there was where the inmates were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surrounded by any sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By barbed wire. Yes. This was all done when we were-- you know, so this is-- we came there, and there was nothing there. So the first few days, as I said. But because of those three pages--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, three pages?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I saw next to me-- I must have fallen asleep, obviously-- I saw three-- three printed pages. Three printed, for me to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know how they-- I don't know. I asked. Nobody wanted to tell me. I don't know, but they got there. And that's what I mean. And suddenly, apparently, the other part in me, you know, your life, or your body, or your [INAUDIBLE], I don't know what it is. The other parts of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is your physical. Physically, physically, I couldn't anymore. But apparently-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is it the soul? Is it the spirit? Or whatever. I recognized that the girls were suffering with-- they were suffering on their own. They were older than I was. You see? But to them, perhaps, straight away, within the next few hours, you know? Even-- even when I gave up, when I looked at them, you already-- just like it happens, you know, with people. You know, something happens, and suddenly, this is not a stranger anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all the time together. We were seven girls in the small bedroom. And somehow, a day, one day, was a long, long time. So after two or three days, this appeared. And then, apparently, you know, I don't remember this exactly, how it happened. But they must have slowly introduced me, you know, that I could-- that I swallowed something. That I-- but I accepted that I'm going on living. I accepted that I'm going on living for now. I don't know, just momentarily, temporarily. I responded today. There was caring there. We were like one person, you know, the seven of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what they were trying to tell me. So I responded to this. Not thanks to them then. But again, I think my interpretation-- I think I'm lucky in the way I interpret my upbringing. And this is where it came to the fore, you know? That if somebody-- if you see kindness, if you see warmth, you respond to it. It means something. It's meaningful. So apparently, at the age of 14 or so, a bit over 14, you know, it comes to you. You respond-- you respond in a way where it's perhaps, basically, primitive. But you know, it's very basic. Those are the very basic feelings and emotions where children like myself were brought up in. So I-- part of me recognized this and responded to this. And this allowed me to respond otherwise as well. Do you remember what the reading material contained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. Nothing, really. It's just that it was printed. I don't remember what it was. It was just very thickly printed. I don't even know if I could read-- if I could read all of this. But it was not-- it was not important what it was about. It was the-- It was the deed itself, that they trusted me. They trusted me to respond. This was my interpretation, that they are trusting me to respond to what they wanted to do, for me to continue to respond to life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you form any strong bonds with any of these seven other women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very strong bonds.

speaker: interviewer
text: With anyone in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think all of them. I think all of them. But I will tell you now, another incident, what-- what happened. All of them saved my life, not literally, but, really, saved my life. I was-- there was-- we went to work in the city for a couple of days. And one inmate escaped. He stayed behind. But he had no chance. He had no chance because of the garb he wore, because he didn't know the language, because of the way he looked. He didn't look nothing that resembled, you know, what you would see a normal person walking down the street. And he was caught a few hours later, and he was hanged in front of us. And we had to-- we were watched if we were watching it. We had to have our eyes open and really watch it. And then, for the next, around, two weeks, it was as before. We had two zahlappells, or three zahlappells, which is a roll call. Then it was one hour inside working, one hour outside standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is what happened for the next 12 days, something like this. And then it was on a Friday. We were peeling [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN]. You know what it is? That's a vegetable. Don't ask me to spell it, please, because it's in German. Kohlrabi, kohlrabi. Perhaps in English, kohlrabi. You don't see this often. It's not very popular. But this is what we had for the soup that we were-- you know. It was in. It was out. It was a bit-- few rotten potatoes or whatever. But because of what has happened, we could not do it where we normally did it, sitting down and preparing. You know? And that was somebody who was preparing to soup or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two of us, we had to do it outside. And I can see it exactly the way it was done. And I was wearing some-- it was wooden clogs, something like this. It was all open. I was wearing a frock with literally nothing underneath. And it was-- I remember the frost. I don't know if it was snowing. I think I was asked this before. I just know it was like frost. I know I was surrounded by frost, so the ground must have been-- must have been partly frozen or whatever. I remember that I was putting out my-- when my feet got too tired or too hot, I was putting them out barefoot to cool them. That's how I remember. And that was on a Friday. And for the last two years, even when I was in Olkusz, every month I was getting very sick. I was getting cramps, and I was feeling ill. And Dr. Lapinski, that was-- this-- his name, I remember. In the ghetto. He was coming--

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Olkusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came a couple of times. And he said, look, that's a young body. She wants to grow. There is no calcium. There is no iron. What do you expect? The body is protesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the-- what were the cramps?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cramps were-- it had to do, you know, with girls. There is fluid in their body that needs to escape. Apparently, what was happening to me was happening for the last two years, but nobody knew about this and nobody really recognized this for what it was. Apparently, when it happened like this, later on it was partly explained to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, before you go on, could you spell the doctor's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Lapinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's L, so you can-- like L-- L-A-P-I-N-S-K-I, Lapinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of what the problem was, what was meant to be happening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just my mother told me that she's surprised that my body does not manifest itself in a certain physical way. Because, you know, we'd had it happen when she was 10 and 1/2. And I was already over 11, or something like this, you know, or 12, and still at 13. And still, you know. But every month, this is what was happening. But the thing was that if it was a medical phenomenon, I don't really know. But what happened that on this particular Friday, after-- this happened right after this young man was hanged. And because of the extra pressure on [INAUDIBLE], or whatever it was, I suddenly felt very ill, and I blew up like a balloon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you blow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- all body, but particularly the stomach. I just-- the fluid somehow, it was so-- I don't remember this part, because apparently, I wasn't conscious or whatever. But there was nothing. In this camp there was-- there was a Dr. Deutsch, who happened to be a-- a Viennese, very famous surgeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Deutsch, like you speak. Like you say Deutsch. German. German in German. Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, we're going to just take a break for a second and change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
